sections.heading: Why concurrency matters
sections.content: In logistics: a delivery-service handling 1000 req/s needs to execute many operations simultaneously. Without concurrency, each request waits for the previous one to finish. Java concurrency model: • Each request in a web server (Tomcat, WildFly) gets its own thread • Threads share the same heap memory (objects accessible across threads) • The JVM + OS schedules threads across CPU cores The danger: Shared mutable state + concurrent access = data corruption, race conditions, deadlocks.

sections.heading: Thread pools — never create raw threads
sections.content:
// ❌ Never in production — uncontrolled thread creation
new Thread(() -> processDelivery(id)).start();
// 1000 requests = 1000 threads = OOM or context-switching hell

// ✅ ExecutorService — bounded, reusable thread pool
ExecutorService executor = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(10);
executor.submit(() -> processDelivery(id));
// 1000 requests share 10 threads — controlled resource usage

// ✅ Spring Boot — inject the managed executor
@Async
public CompletableFuture<Route> calculateRouteAsync(Delivery d) {
    return CompletableFuture.completedFuture(routeService.calculate(d));
}

// ✅ Java EE — ManagedExecutorService (container-managed)
@Resource
ManagedExecutorService executor;
executor.submit(() -> processDelivery(id));
Thread pool sizing rule of thumb: • CPU-bound tasks: threads = number of CPU cores • I/O-bound tasks (REST calls, DB): threads = cores × (1 + wait_time/compute_time) • Example: 8 cores, tasks spend 80% waiting on I/O → 8 × (1 + 0.8/0.2) = 40 threads

sections.heading: CompletableFuture basics
Parallel calls pattern (REWE transport)
sections.content:
// Scenario: dispatch requires data from 3 services
// Sequential: 100ms + 150ms + 200ms = 450ms
// Parallel:   max(100, 150, 200) = 200ms!

public DispatchResponse dispatch(Long deliveryId) {
    var driverFuture = CompletableFuture
        .supplyAsync(() -> driverService.findAvailable(region), executor);
    var routeFuture = CompletableFuture
        .supplyAsync(() -> routeService.optimize(stops), executor);
    var weatherFuture = CompletableFuture
        .supplyAsync(() -> weatherService.getForecast(region), executor);

    // Wait for all three (with timeout!)
    CompletableFuture.allOf(driverFuture, routeFuture, weatherFuture)
        .orTimeout(5, TimeUnit.SECONDS)
        .join();

    return new DispatchResponse(
        driverFuture.join(),
        routeFuture.join(),
        weatherFuture.join()
    );
}
Key rules: • ALWAYS pass an executor (never use default ForkJoinPool in a server) • ALWAYS set a timeout (orTimeout or completeOnTimeout) • Handle exceptions with exceptionally() or handle() • Don't block the calling thread unnecessarily (use thenApply/thenCompose for chaining)

sections.heading: Race conditions and how to prevent them
sections.content:
// ❌ Race condition — two threads read-then-write simultaneously
class DeliveryCounter {
    private int count = 0;
    public void increment() { count++; } // NOT atomic! read → add → write
}
// Thread A reads 5, Thread B reads 5, both write 6 → lost update!

// ✅ Fix 1: AtomicInteger (lock-free)
private AtomicInteger count = new AtomicInteger(0);
public void increment() { count.incrementAndGet(); }

// ✅ Fix 2: synchronized (simple but coarse)
public synchronized void increment() { count++; }

// ✅ Fix 3: Immutable objects (no shared mutable state)
record DeliveryEvent(String id, String status, Instant time) {}
// Records are immutable — safe to share across threads without locks
Best strategies (in order of preference): 1. Avoid sharing: each thread works on its own data (request-scoped) 2. Immutability: objects that can't change are always thread-safe 3. Atomic operations: AtomicInteger, AtomicReference, ConcurrentHashMap 4. Synchronized/Lock: last resort (risk of deadlocks, contention)

sections.heading: Common thread-safety patterns
sections.content:
// ConcurrentHashMap — thread-safe map (BUT not for compound operations)
ConcurrentHashMap<String, Integer> cache = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

// ❌ NOT atomic (check-then-act race condition)
if (!cache.containsKey(key)) {
    cache.put(key, computeValue()); // another thread might insert between check and put
}

// ✅ Atomic compound operation
cache.computeIfAbsent(key, k -> computeValue());

// ✅ Spring: singleton beans are thread-safe IF they have no mutable fields
@Service
public class DeliveryService {
    private final DeliveryRepository repo; // final = safe
    // NO mutable instance fields!
}

// ❌ Dangerous: mutable field in singleton
@Service
public class BadService {
    private List<String> buffer = new ArrayList<>(); // shared across ALL requests!
}
Interview answer: "In Spring Boot, services are singletons shared across threads. I ensure thread safety by: (1) using only final immutable fields, (2) keeping mutable state in method-local variables, (3) using atomic operations for shared counters/caches."

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content:
Senior engineers know that concurrency bugs are the hardest to reproduce and the most dangerous in production.

Key distinctions:
• Thread safety is not about synchronized — it's about managing shared mutable state
• A race condition may not manifest in testing but appear under production load
• Thread pools are bounded resources — exhausting them cascades to the entire system
• CompletableFuture composes well but error handling is non-obvious
• Virtual Threads (Java 21) eliminate most reasons to use reactive programming for I/O-bound work

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content:
In TRAB's high-throughput delivery processing:

• Route optimization uses parallel streams for CPU-bound segment calculations
• Delivery event consumers run with controlled parallelism (Kafka maxPollRecords + thread pool)
• External API calls (weather, traffic) use CompletableFuture with timeout to avoid blocking
• Connection pool exhaustion is the #1 concurrency incident — caused by holding connections during external calls
• Virtual Threads would simplify the codebase by eliminating reactive wrappers for blocking JDBC

sections.heading: When NOT to use
sections.content:
When NOT to add concurrency:

• When the operation is I/O-bound on a single resource (adding threads just creates contention)
• When the code is already fast enough sequentially
• When debugging complexity outweighs performance gain
• When the shared state is too complex to reason about safely
• When you can solve the problem with batching or caching instead of parallelism

sections.heading: Why synchronization exists
synchronized — the simplest lock
sections.content:
// synchronized method — locks on 'this'
public synchronized void increment() {
    value++;
}

// synchronized block — explicit monitor object
public void process(Order order) {
    synchronized (this) {
        // Only one thread at a time
        orders.add(order);
        total += order.amount();
    }
    // Non-critical work here — no lock held
    notifyListeners(order); // outside the lock!
}

// synchronized on a dedicated lock object (preferred for encapsulation)
private final Object lock = new Object();

public void addDelivery(Delivery d) {
    synchronized (lock) {
        deliveries.add(d);
    }
}
Rules: • Keep the synchronized block as small as possible — only the critical section • Never call external code or I/O while holding a lock (deadlock and latency risk) • Locking on this leaks the monitor — prefer a private lock object

sections.heading: ReentrantLock — explicit control
ReadWriteLock — optimise read-heavy workloads
Deadlock — the silent killer
When to use what
sections.content:
// Decision guide:

// 1. No shared mutable state? → No lock needed (best option)
record DeliveryEvent(String id, Instant time) {} // immutable

// 2. Single variable counter/flag? → Atomic classes
AtomicInteger activeDeliveries = new AtomicInteger(0);

// 3. Simple critical section? → synchronized
public synchronized void process(Delivery d) { ... }

// 4. Need tryLock / timeout / interrupt? → ReentrantLock
if (lock.tryLock(100, MILLISECONDS)) { ... }

// 5. Read-heavy cache/registry? → ReadWriteLock
rwLock.readLock().lock(); // many readers simultaneously

// 6. Very high-read throughput? → StampedLock optimistic
long stamp = sl.tryOptimisticRead(); ...
Senior rule: always start with the simplest option that is thread-safe. Add lock complexity only when profiling confirms contention is the bottleneck. Prefer ConcurrentHashMap, CopyOnWriteArrayList, and java.util.concurrent over manual locking — they are already battle-tested.

sections.heading: Production context
sections.content: In a delivery platform like REWE TRAB: • A route assignment cache uses ReadWriteLock — 95% of accesses are reads, writes happen only on route changes • A delivery counter for metrics uses AtomicLong — simple increment, no contention overhead • A batch job coordinator uses ReentrantLock with tryLock to prevent two pods from claiming the same batch • Dead-lock prevention through lock ordering applies to the account-credit flow in the financial module Monitoring: Lock contention appears in thread dumps as BLOCKED state. If many threads are BLOCKED waiting for the same monitor, that class is a bottleneck. Use jstack or async-profiler to detect it before users notice latency spikes.

sections.heading: Interview defense points
sections.content: Q: What is the difference between synchronized and ReentrantLock? Both provide mutual exclusion. synchronized is simpler but limited — you cannot time out or interrupt a waiting thread. ReentrantLock adds tryLock(), lockInterruptibly(), and fairness. In practice, synchronized is sufficient for most use cases. Q: How do you prevent deadlocks? Three strategies: consistent lock ordering (most reliable), lock timeout with tryLock and backoff, and avoiding nested locking entirely. The best prevention is designing code so that nested locks are unnecessary. Q: When would you use ReadWriteLock? When reads vastly outnumber writes and the critical section is non-trivial. For simple counters, AtomicLong is faster. For maps, ConcurrentHashMap handles concurrent reads internally. ReadWriteLock shines for custom data structures with complex read operations.

sections.heading: Why the JMM exists
sections.content: Modern CPUs and JIT compilers reorder instructions and cache values in CPU registers to improve performance. This works perfectly for single-threaded code. In multi-threaded code it breaks everything. Without a memory model contract, the JVM could: • Cache a variable's value in a CPU register and never flush it to RAM • Reorder two independent write operations for performance • Let one thread see a partially-constructed object from another thread The Java Memory Model (JMM) — defined in JSR-133, formalised in Java 5 — specifies the rules for when writes become visible to other threads. It is the foundation every Java concurrency primitive is built on.

sections.heading: The happens-before relationship
Visibility — the problem volatile solves
Ordering — instruction reordering
Memory barriers
sections.content:
Happens-before relationships are implemented at the CPU level using memory barriers (also called memory fences). A memory barrier is a CPU instruction that prevents certain reorderings across the barrier point. Four types of barriers (conceptual): | Barrier | Prevents | |---------|----------| | LoadLoad | Loads before barrier complete before loads after | | StoreStore | Stores before barrier complete before stores after | | LoadStore | Loads before barrier complete before stores after | | StoreLoad | Stores before barrier complete before loads after (most expensive) | Practical mapping in Java: 
// volatile read → LoadLoad + LoadStore barrier after
// volatile write → StoreStore + StoreLoad barrier before
// synchronized entry → LoadLoad + LoadStore after acquiring lock
// synchronized exit → StoreStore + StoreLoad before releasing lock
Why this matters: volatile is cheaper than synchronized because it avoids mutual exclusion — it only needs memory visibility, not atomicity. But it still has a cost (StoreLoad is expensive on x86). Measure before using volatile as a performance optimisation.

sections.heading: Safe publication
Practical implications
sections.content:
When to use volatile: • Simple flags: volatile boolean running, volatile boolean initialized • Publishing an immutable object reference: volatile ImmutableConfig config • Single writer, multiple readers — no compound operations When volatile is NOT enough: 
// ❌ volatile does NOT make this atomic
private volatile int counter = 0;
public void increment() { counter++; } // Still: read → add → write (3 ops)
// Two threads can both read 5 and both write 6

// ✅ Use AtomicInteger instead
private AtomicInteger counter = new AtomicInteger(0);
public void increment() { counter.incrementAndGet(); } // Atomic CAS operation
The visibility triangle: • volatile → visibility only, no mutual exclusion • synchronized → visibility + mutual exclusion • AtomicXxx → visibility + atomic compound operations (CAS) Choose the weakest guarantee that is correct for your use case.

sections.heading: Interview defense points
sections.content: Q: What is happens-before? A happens-before relationship means that if A happens-before B, all side effects of A are guaranteed visible to B. It is the JMM's way of defining memory visibility contracts without specifying CPU architectures. Every Java synchronization primitive creates happens-before edges. Q: What does volatile guarantee? Two things: visibility (writes are immediately flushed to main memory, reads always go to main memory) and ordering (volatile writes cannot be reordered with preceding writes; volatile reads cannot be reordered with subsequent reads). It does NOT guarantee atomicity of compound operations. Q: Is the double-checked locking pattern safe? Yes, but only since Java 5, and only when the field is declared volatile. The volatile write prevents the JVM from reordering the reference assignment before field initialisation, which is the classic failure mode on older JVMs. Q: What is safe publication? An object is safely published when other threads are guaranteed to see it in a fully initialised state. The four safe publication idioms are: final fields, volatile references, atomic references, and synchronised access. Unsafe publication — publishing a reference without synchronisation — can expose partially constructed objects.
